		<title>Near-memory Dequantization Architecture In Custom HBM for LLM inference (SK hynix)</title>
		<link>https://semiengineering.com/near-memory-dequantization-architecture-in-custom-hbm-for-llm-inference-sk-hynix/</link>

		<guid isPermaLink="false">https://semiengineering.com/?p=24278842</guid>

					<description><![CDATA[<p>Researchers from SK hynix published a technical paper titled “StreamDQ: Near-Memory Weight DeQuantization in Custom HBM for Scalable AI Inference Acceleration.” The paper proposes StreamDQ for “a lightweight architectural enhancement that enables on-the-fly dequantization in the memory subsystem for high-throughput, large-batch LLM inference,” and reports “up to 7.08× speedup and 90.23% lower energy” for mixed-precision... <a class="read_more" href="https://semiengineering.com/near-memory-dequantization-architecture-in-custom-hbm-for-llm-inference-sk-hynix/">&#187; read more</a></p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/near-memory-dequantization-architecture-in-custom-hbm-for-llm-inference-sk-hynix/">Near-memory Dequantization Architecture In Custom HBM for LLM inference (SK hynix)</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<p>Researchers from SK hynix published a technical paper titled “StreamDQ: Near-Memory Weight DeQuantization in Custom HBM for Scalable AI Inference Acceleration.”</p>
<p>The paper proposes StreamDQ for “a lightweight architectural enhancement that enables on-the-fly dequantization in the memory subsystem for high-throughput, large-batch LLM inference,” and reports “up to 7.08× speedup and 90.23% lower energy” for mixed-precision GEMM.</p>
<p>Find the technical paper <a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.08993">here</a>. July 2026.</p>
<p>Jeong, Minki, Daegun Yoon, Soohong Ahn, Seungyong Lee, Nameun Kang, Hyeonseok Ju, Ieryung Park, Joonseop Sim, Youngpyo Joo, and Hoshik Kim. “StreamDQ: Near-Memory Weight DeQuantization in Custom HBM for Scalable AI Inference Acceleration.” arXiv, July 2026. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2607.08993.</p>
<p>&nbsp;</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/near-memory-dequantization-architecture-in-custom-hbm-for-llm-inference-sk-hynix/">Near-memory Dequantization Architecture In Custom HBM for LLM inference (SK hynix)</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></content:encoded>

					<description><![CDATA[<p>Plenty of cash for AI and its enablers; 80 startups raise over $6B.</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/startup-funding-q2-2026/">Startup Funding: Q2 2026</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<p>Investors kept pouring money into AI hardware startups in the second quarter of 2026. While companies focused on chips for AI data centers have largely dominated the funding over the past year, startups creating edge silicon re-emerged this quarter as investors see the appeal of physical AI and real-time on-device applications. However, large-scale AI infrastructure chips and other attendant aspects, such as faster data movement and higher voltage power semiconductors, still drew the bulk of the AI funding.</p>
<p>Quantum, too, had a particularly successful quarter, with 21 companies raising funds, six of which drew $100 million or more. Different qubit modalities were well represented, with superconducting hardware joined by spin-based, neural atom, and ion trap approaches. Cryogenic control electronics, quantum chip test, and networking also saw funding.</p>
<p>There were a plethora of large rounds during the quarter: 18 companies reached or exceeded $100 million, including numerous early-stage rounds. The quarter also saw innovative inspection technologies, a new Malaysian OSAT, a fresh infusion of government cash for Rapidus, and much more in this look at 80 companies that collectively raised over $6 billion in Q2 2026.</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="#Table">Summary table</a></li>
<li><a href="#Chips">Chips</a></li>
<li><a href="#AI-hardware">AI hardware</a></li>
<li><a href="#EDA">EDA &amp; design</a></li>

<li><a href="#Photonics">Photonics &amp; optics</a></li>
<li><a href="#Sensors">Sensors</a></li>
<li><a href="https://semiengineering.com/business-news-startups/">Previous reports</a></li>
</ul>
<h2 id="Chips">Chips</h2>
<p><strong>SiFive</strong> raised <a href="https://www.sifive.com/press/sifive-raises-400-million-to-accelerate-high-performance-risc-v-data-center-solutions">$400.0M in Series G funding</a> led by <strong>Atreides Management</strong> and joined by <strong>Apollo Global Management</strong>, <strong>Nvidia</strong>, <strong>Point72 Turion</strong>, <strong>T. Rowe Price Investment Management</strong>, <strong>Prosperity7 Ventures</strong>, and <strong>Sutter Hill Ventures</strong>. SiFive provides a broad range of RISC-V-based processor core IP. Its portfolio consists of high-performance 64-bit application processors with between 3-wide to 6-wide out-of-order cores and up to 2 dedicated vector engines; pre-defined and customizable low-power embedded microcontrollers; highly scalable vector and matrix processors for AI and modern workloads, which include vector operations specifically tuned to accelerate machine learning operations; and deterministic real-time processor solutions specifically designed for the automotive industry. Funds will accelerate the development of RISC-V CPU and AI IP solutions for the data center. Founded in 2015, it is based in Santa Clara, California, USA.</p>
<p><strong>AttoTude</strong> raised <a href="https://www.attotude.com/news-updates-posts/seriesc">$52.0M in Series C funding</a> led by <strong>The Westly Group</strong>, joined by <strong>Keysight</strong>, <strong>Allegis Capital</strong>, <strong>DNX Ventures</strong>, <strong>Sutter Hill Ventures</strong>, <strong>Mayfield</strong>, <strong>Canaan Partners</strong>, and <strong>Wing Venture Capital</strong>. AttoTude offers an interconnect platform for AI and hyperscale infrastructure that combines advanced ASIC signal generation with low-loss dielectric waveguides to enable high-bandwidth, energy-efficient connectivity for per-lane data rates of 200G, 400G, and 800G. The THz radio over wire approach aims to deliver scalable mid-range connectivity that overcomes the performance limitations of copper while avoiding the complexity of optical technologies. Funds will support the build-out of the first prototypes and supply chain establishment. Founded in 2024, it is based in Menlo Park, California, USA, and has raised $143M to date.</p>
<p><strong>HYFIX Spatial Intelligence</strong> raised <a href="https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/hyfix-announces-15m-seed-round-to-build-american-made-chips-powering-the-next-generation-of-drones-and-robots-302743129.html">$15.0M in seed funding</a> led by <strong>Craft Ventures</strong>, with participation from <strong>Catapult Ventures</strong>, <strong>Multicoin Capital</strong>, <strong>Finality Capital</strong>, and <strong>Sky Dayton</strong>. HYFIX is designing an SoC for <a href="https://hyfix.ai/">drones and robotics</a> that integrates flight control, high-accuracy positioning, secure wireless communications, and onboard intelligence. It is designed to operate reliably in GPS-degraded or denied environments and supports open-source ecosystems including ROS 2, ArduPilot, and PX4. It targets a range of drone sizes and weights across consumer, commercial, and defense markets. Founded in 2022, it is based in Santa Clara, California, USA.</p>
<p><strong>BigEndian Semiconductors</strong> raised <a href="https://www.vertexventures.sg/news/bigendian-semiconductors-raises-6m-to-strengthen-india-s-fabless-semiconductor-ecosystem/">$6.0M in funding</a> led by <strong>IAN Group</strong>, joined by <strong>Vertex Ventures SEA &amp; India</strong>, <strong>IvyCap Ventures</strong>, and angel investors. BigEndian is developing high-performance <a href="https://bigendiansemi.com">surveillance camera SoCs</a> for enterprise and consumer applications, with a focus on manageability and a flexible design approach. The startup recently taped out its first chip and will use the funds to move from test silicon to real-world deployments. Founded in 2024, it is based in Bengaluru, India.</p>
<p><strong>Morphing Machines</strong> added <a href="https://www.linkedin.com/posts/morphing-machines_inc42-news-startups-activity-7454824680122736640-wfbI/">INR 420.0M (~$4.4M) to its Series A round</a> from <strong>Navam Capital</strong>, <strong>Hero Enterprise</strong>, and <strong>Colossa Ventures</strong>. Morphing Machines develops a many-core, massively parallel, <a href="https://www.morphing.in">runtime reconfigurable SoC platform</a> that can scale from 16 cores to 4K cores. The embedded processor platform enables multiple domain-specific architectures on a single chip that can be instantiated on demand upon an event to handle mixed critical tasks and applications simultaneously. Target markets include avionics, automotive, and 5G/6G telecom, as well as data center, AI infrastructure, and HPC. Funds will support a test chip and customer pilots. Founded in 2005, it is based in Bengaluru, India.</p>
<h2 id="AI-hardware">AI hardware</h2>
<p><strong>Etched </strong>emerged from stealth with <a href="https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/06/30/3319922/0/en/etched-emerges-from-stealth-with-working-chip-800m-raised-and-over-1b-in-customer-contracts.html">$500.0M in funding</a> from <strong>VentureTech Alliance</strong>, <strong>Jane Street</strong>, <strong>Hudson River Trading</strong>, <strong>Jump Trading</strong>, <strong>Two Sigma</strong>, <strong>Stripes</strong>, <strong>Ribbit Capital</strong>, <strong>Radical Ventures</strong>, <strong>Primary VC</strong>, <strong>Positive Sum</strong>, and individual investors. Etched co-designs chips, racks, software, and manufacturing methods for <a href="https://www.etched.com/">frontier model inference</a>. The startup has designed an architecture that can operate the chip&#8217;s math blocks at reduced voltage, which it says enables it to run trillion-parameter sparse MoEs at 80%+ peak FLOPs without thermal throttling. Etched also uses a hybrid HBM/SRAM design with a shared memory pool, enabled by an ultra-low-latency, high-bandwidth interconnect to speed memory access across chips. The company plans to ship rack-scale systems this summer. Founded in 2022, it is based in San Jose, California, USA, and has raised $800M to date.</p>
<p><strong>Acrab</strong> emerged from stealth with <a href="https://vertexholdings.com/news/acrab-raises-over-us-350m-to-build-the-compute-foundation-for-agentic-ai/">over $350.0M in funding</a> from <strong>Vertex Ventures SEA &amp; India</strong> and others. Acrab is developing a full-stack compute architecture for <a href="https://acrab.ai">edge agentic AI</a>, spanning AI silicon, local LLM inference, an operating system, multimodal human-machine interfaces, and agent orchestration. The startup is designing its SoC for on-device inference with a focus on improving the coordination between CPUs and NPUs in a heterogeneous system. Funds will support R&amp;D. Founded in 2024, it is based in Singapore.</p>
<p><strong>Fractile</strong> raised <a href="https://www.fractile.ai/news/fractile-raises-220m-to-build-the-next-generation-of-inference-hardware">$220.0M in funding</a> led by <strong>Accel Partners</strong>, <strong>Factorial Funds</strong>, and <strong>Founders Fund</strong>, with participation from <strong>Conviction Partners</strong>, <strong>Gigascale Capital</strong>, <strong>O1A</strong>, <strong>Felicis</strong>, <strong>Buckley Ventures</strong>, <strong>8VC</strong>, and existing investors. Fractile develops frontier LLM inference chips where memory and compute are physically interleaved. The startup claims its approach can enable longer context windows and serve thousands of tokens per second to thousands of concurrent users within a reasonable power budget. Funds will support delivery of its first chips. Founded in 2022, it is based in London, UK.</p>
<p><strong>Upscale AI</strong> added <a href="https://upscale.com/blogs/upscale-ai-adds-190-million-in-extension-to-series-a-reaching-half-billion-dollars-in-total-funding">$190.0M in a Series A-1 round</a> led by <strong>Premji Invest</strong>, joined by <strong>NVIDIA</strong>, <strong>Salesforce Ventures</strong>, <strong>Seligman Ventures</strong>, <strong>Temasek</strong>, <strong>Maverick Silicon</strong>, <strong>Mayfield</strong>, <strong>Prosperity7 Ventures</strong>, <strong>StepStone Group</strong>, and <strong>Tiger Global</strong>. Upscale AI provides silicon, systems, and software for ultra-low AI latency networking. Based around open standards such as ESUN, Ultra Accelerator Link, and Ultra Ethernet, the company&#8217;s platform unifies GPUs, AI accelerators, memory, storage, and networking into a single, synchronized AI engine. Offerings include a high-bandwidth AI networking fabric for data centers, a network operating system based on SAI/SONiC, and rack platforms. Founded in 2025, it is based in Santa Clara, California, USA, and has raised $500M to date.</p>
<p><strong>XCENA</strong> raised <a href="https://xcena.com/newsroom/?q=YToxOntzOjEyOiJrZXl3b3JkX3R5cGUiO3M6MzoiYWxsIjt9&amp;bmode=view&amp;idx=171571166&amp;t=board">$135.0M in Series B funding</a> led by <strong>Atinum Investment</strong> and <strong>IMM Investment</strong>, with participation from <strong>SBI Investment</strong>, <strong>Mirae Asset Capital</strong>, <strong>STIC Ventures</strong>, <strong>Wonik Investment Partners</strong>, <strong>SV Investment</strong>, <strong>LB Investment</strong>, <strong>Corstone Asia</strong>, <strong>Kiwoom Investment</strong>, <strong>DSC Investment</strong>, <strong>Shinhan Venture Investment</strong>, <strong>Korea Development Bank</strong>, <strong>KDB Capital</strong>, <strong>Premier Partners</strong>, <strong>Kolon Investment</strong>, <strong>Company K Partners</strong>, <strong>K2 Investment Partners</strong>, <strong>Partners Investment</strong>, and <strong>Kyobo Securities</strong>. XCENA develops computational memory architectures that reduce data bottlenecks in AI infrastructure and other high-demand compute workloads. Built on CXL 3.2, the approach merges high-capacity pooled DDR5 memory with near-data processing comprised of thousands of custom RISC-V cores and vector processing engines to support data orchestration tasks, including preprocessing and KV cache management. The company also provides a full-stack SDK with multi-level APIs, simulation tools, and drivers. Funds will be used to validate real-world performance, scale deployments globally, and expand go-to-market capabilities. Founded in 2022 as MetisX, it is based in Seongnam, South Korea, and has raised $185M to date.</p>
<p><strong>Dnotitia</strong> raised <a href="https://world.einnews.com/pr_news/906864141/dnotitia-closes-krw-90-billion-series-a-to-accelerate-ai-storage-expansion">KRW 90.0B (~$61.2M) in a Series A round</a> led by <strong>Elohim Partners</strong>, with participation from <strong>Kiwoom Investment</strong>, <strong>Starting Line</strong>, <strong>Maple Investment Partners</strong>, <strong>Daesung Startup Investment</strong>, <strong>Shinhan Venture Investment</strong>, <strong>Ulmus Investment</strong>, <strong>KOLON Investment</strong>, <strong>HB Investment</strong>, <strong>Tony Investment</strong>, <strong>SJ Investment Partners</strong>, and <strong>FuturePlay</strong>. Dnotitia is developing a vector database and <a href="https://dnotitia.com/">vector data processing unit</a> (VDPU) designed to accelerate data search and processing in generative AI environments. The VDPU is part of the company&#8217;s vision for a unified AI data stack that integrates external knowledge, long-term memory, and working memory. It is also working on a personal device for running LLMs. Founded in 2023, it is based in Seoul, South Korea.</p>
<p><strong>HrdWyr</strong> raised <a href="https://www.linkedin.com/posts/hrdwyr-company-news-ugcPost-7460285890892861441-ZlzT/">$13.0M in Series A funding</a> led by <strong>Ideaspring Capital</strong>, with participation from <strong>Singularity AMC</strong>, <strong>Avatar Growth Capital</strong>, and <strong>Persistent Systems</strong>. HrdWyr develops <a href="https://www.hrdwyr.com/">domain-specific AI SoCs</a>. The chip architecture combines real-time processing with adaptive learning, implementing a dedicated AI engine that enables smart decision-making and parallel processing, while ML evolves and optimizes the chip based on real-world usage patterns. Target applications include consumer electronics, white goods, EVs, and data centers. Founded in 2023, it is based in Bengaluru, India.</p>
<h2 id="EDA">EDA &amp; design</h2>
<p><strong>Cognichip</strong> raised <a href="https://www.cognichip.ai/news/seligman-ventures-leads-cognichips-60m-series-a-to-back-physics-informed-ai-for-chip-design-intel-ceo-lip-bu-tan-and-seligman-ventures-umesh-padval-join-the-board">$60.0M in Series A funding</a> led by <strong>Seligman Ventures</strong>, with participation from <strong>SBI Investment</strong>, <strong>Mayfield</strong>, <strong>Lux Capital</strong>, <strong>FPV</strong>, <strong>Candou Ventures</strong>, and others. Cognichip has created a physics-informed AI foundation model for chip design that fuses logic and physics-based reasoning to navigate complex design spaces and provide unified planning and orchestration, unlimited context management, predictable operational efficiency, and secure deployment into chip design environments. By shifting from traditional serial workflows to concurrent, adaptive, and highly automated design processes, the startup claims its full-stack AI-enabled design solution can reduce design cycles, cut development costs, and optimize designs for power, performance, and efficiency. Founded in 2024, it is based in Redwood City, California, USA, and has raised $93M to date.</p>
<p><strong>Architect Labs</strong> emerged from stealth with <a href="https://architectlabs.com/blog/seed">$24.0M in seed funding</a> led by <strong>Kindred Ventures</strong>, with participation from <strong>Race Capital</strong>, <strong>TQ Ventures</strong>, and <strong>Together Fund</strong>. Architect Labs is building an end-to-end AI system that it claims can design and provably verify custom chips for a client&#8217;s specification or target workload. Funds will be used for compute infrastructure, AI research, and to co-design production silicon with early partners. Founded in 2025, it is based in Palo Alto, California, USA.</p>
<p><strong>BoolSi </strong>raised <a href="https://boolsi.com/seed-announcement.html">$6.0M in seed funding</a> led by <strong>Fine Structure Ventures</strong>, with participation from <strong>Pillar VC</strong>, <strong>Fifth Quarter Ventures</strong>, and <strong>Coalition Ventures</strong>. BoolSi is developing a compiler that can take embedded software written in C, C++, or other high-level languages and custom-generate a circuit and driver for an FPGA accelerator that sits next to the CPU to address targeted bottlenecks. To achieve this, the startup says it has developed neural networks that are a strict superset of the circuits that can implement a program&#8217;s behavior, where training drives the weights toward exact discrete values until it converges into digital circuits. Functional and formal verification are employed to check correctness. Its initial target market is embedded developers in robotics. Founded in 2023, it is based in Boston, Massachusetts, USA.</p>
<h2 id="Manufacturing-equipment">Manufacturing &amp; equipment</h2>
<p><strong>Rapidus</strong> received an additional <a href="https://www.rapidus.inc/en/news_topics/information/20260605-2/">JPY 150.0B (~$943.0M) subsidy</a> from Japan&#8217;s <strong>Information-Technology Promotion Agency</strong>, following an investment earlier this year. Rapidus is a pure-play foundry that will offer leading-edge process technology and advanced packaging in an integrated, end-to-end approach. The company is currently building a 2nm logic semiconductor fab, with mass production expected to begin in 2027. Founded in 2022, it is based in Tokyo, Japan.</p>
<p><strong>Syenta</strong> raised <a href="https://www.syenta.com/news-and-research/pat-gelsinger-joins-syentas-board-as-playground-global-and-nrf-lead-a-37m-raise">AUD $37.0M (~$26.0M) in Series A funding</a> led by <strong>Playground Global</strong> and Australia&#8217;s <strong>National Reconstruction Fund</strong>, with participation from <strong>Investible</strong>, <strong>Salus Ventures</strong>, <strong>Jelix Ventures</strong>, and <strong>Wollemi Capital</strong>. Syenta has developed localized electrochemical manufacturing technology that combines deposition and patterning in a single step to build high-resolution chip-to-chip interconnects. The method uses a stamp electrode with a dielectric pattern that defines localized electrochemical cells, enabling precision metal deposition only within those cells. Syenta says its approach enables sub-micron interconnects manufactured on large &gt;1000 mm<sup>2</sup> packages with high throughput. Founded in 2022, it is based in Sydney, Australia.</p>
<p><strong>Itera</strong> emerged from stealth with <a href="https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260527247911/en/Itera-Exits-Stealth-With-%2412M-to-Bring-Real-Time-Prototyping-to-Electronics">$12.0M in seed funding</a> from <strong>Upfront Ventures</strong>, <strong>Costanoa Ventures</strong>, and <strong>Colle Capital</strong>. Itera has developed a <a href="https://www.itera.co/">fluid circuit board</a> testing and prototyping system based around a glass and liquid metal substrate that can be reconfigured in under 60 seconds. The startup&#8217;s business model has companies provide it with design files, which it compiles on the substrate. Itera then provides secure remote access to the circuit board, with complete circuit visibility, for testing and iteration, delivering production-ready design files at the end of the process. Founded in 2024, it is based in San Francisco, California, USA.</p>
<h2 id="Test-measurement-inspection">Test, measurement &amp; inspection</h2>
<p><strong>Nearfield Instruments</strong> raised <a href="https://www.nearfieldinstruments.com/portfolio/380-million-series-d-funding/">$380.0M in Series D funding</a> led by <strong>Fidelity Management &amp; Research Company</strong>, along with <strong>Temasek</strong>, <strong>Walden Catalyst Ventures</strong>, <strong>Innovation Industries</strong>, <strong>M&amp;G Investments</strong>, <strong>Invest-NL</strong>, <strong>Qatar Investment Authority</strong>, <strong>TNO Ventures</strong>, and <strong>ING Group</strong>. Nearfield Instruments supplies inspection and metrology equipment. Its 3D scanning probe microscopy system can perform non-destructive in-line measurement of 3nm and below gate-all-around FETs, complementary FETs, and hybrid-bonded structures, including sidewall measurements of complex structures such as high-aspect-ratio trenches, GAA recess, vias, and multi-layered stacks. Nearfield&#8217;s equipment uses a parallel architecture of miniature SPMs to throughput, while the sidewall imaging mode combines sidewall force sensing and advanced sensor fusion to determine the probe&#8217;s precise position in 3D space. Founded in 2016 as a spin off from research organization TNO, it is based in Rotterdam, the Netherlands.</p>
<p><strong>Liquid Instruments</strong> raised <a href="https://liquidinstruments.com/news-updates/series-c/?utm_campaign=series-c&amp;utm_source=alert_banner&amp;utm_medium=banner">$50.0M in Series C funding</a> led by <strong>Keysight Technologies</strong> and Australia&#8217;s <strong>National Reconstruction Fund Corporation</strong>. Liquid Instruments makes software-defined test and measurement devices that use FPGAs to combine multiple instruments into one. The reconfigurable platform includes oscilloscope, programmable power supply, PID controller, digital logic analyzer, arbitrary waveform generator, data logger, spectrum analyzer, and more. The company&#8217;s offerings range from portable devices for quick experiments and testing to high-bandwidth advanced devices that allow users to run and connect multiple instruments at the same time for a customized test system. Applications include aerospace and defense, semiconductor, and quantum research. Funds will be used to accelerate product development, scale the platform, and expand go-to-market in key verticals. Founded in 2014, it is based in Canberra, Australia.</p>
<p><strong>Invisix</strong> raised <a href="https://invisix.com/blog/invisix-raises-e20m-seed-round-to-bring-soft-x-ray-metrology-to-ai-era-chip-manufacturing/">€20.0M (~$23.3M) in seed funding</a> from <strong>Hitachi Ventures</strong>, <strong>Transition Ventures</strong>, <strong>imec.xpand</strong>, <strong>Doosan Investment</strong>, and others. Invisix offers a high-throughput, non-destructive metrology system using soft X-ray scatterometry. Its High Harmonic Generation (HHG) process uses a short-pulsed drive laser to excite noble-gas atoms into a high-energy state, in which the atoms emit short-wavelength soft X-ray light at many colors, generating a richer 3D signal than typical single-wavelength lasers. This is combined with reconstruction algorithms and machine learning to reconstruct the detailed 3D internal structure of devices, with scalable throughput for high-volume manufacturing. The technology has been used to successfully measure key features in gate-all-around transistors. Funds will be used to accelerate development of its first shippable system, support customer demonstrations, and hire. Founded in 2025 as a spin-out from ASML, it is based in Eindhoven, the Netherlands.</p>
<p><strong>FusionAP</strong> raised <a href="https://www.vertexventures.sg/news/fusionap-raises-us-2-million-pre-seed-to-advance-malaysia-s-push-into-advanced-semiconductor-packaging/">$2.0M in pre-seed funding</a> led by <strong>Vertex Ventures SEA &amp; India</strong> and <strong>Southern Capital Group</strong>. FusionAP is building a geopolitically neutral <a href="https://fusionap.com/">outsourced semiconductor assembly and test</a> (OSAT) platform for advanced 2.5D, 3D, and beyond-Moore packaging solutions combining chiplet architectures, advanced interconnects, and system-level optimization. Funds will go towards engineering and process integration build-out, R&amp;D and intellectual property development, and pilot capacity. Founded in 2025, it is based in Penang, Malaysia.</p>
<h2 id="Analog-mixed-signal">Analog &amp; mixed signal</h2>
<p><strong>Stathera</strong> raised <a href="https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/stathera-announces-us55-million-series-b-to-scale-silicon-timing-and-accelerate-next-generation-ai-data-center-solutions-302813712.html">$55.0M in Series B funding</a> led by <strong>Maverick Silicon</strong> with participation from <strong>Celesta Capital</strong>, <strong>BDC Capital</strong>, <strong>MediaTek Innovation Fund</strong>, <strong>TXC Corporation</strong>, and <strong>Ultratech Capital Partners</strong>. Stathera develops <a href="https://www.stathera.com">MEMS-based timing solutions</a> that provide both MHz and kHz reference clock, enabling it to replace two quartz oscillators. Along with a low-jitter packaged MEMS oscillator, Stathera also offers an embedded MEMS resonator that can be integrated into an MCU/SoC/PMIC for system control, power-moding, and Real Time Clock (RTC), or into communication ICs. The startup says its technology is resistant to temperature changes, vibration, and shock with low power consumption and area, making it suitable for applications such as wearables, IoT, smartphones, and other wireless connected devices. Funds will be used for mass production of its GEN2 32.768 kHz product and development of its next-gen dual-mode platform for AI data centers. Founded in 2020 based on technology that originated at McGill University and was further developed by Nxtsens, it is based in Montreal, Quebec, Canada, and has raised $75M to date.</p>
<p><strong>Point2 Technology</strong> added <a href="https://point2tech.com/point2-secures-investment-from-maverick-silicon-with-participation-from-nventures-and-umc/">$31.4M to its Series B round</a> led by <strong>Maverick Silicon</strong>, with participation from Nvidia&#8217;s <strong>NVentures</strong> and <strong>UMC Capital</strong>. Point2 develops ultra‑low‑power, ultra‑low‑latency RF and mixed‑signal interconnect SoCs and smart retimers for scalable, high‑bandwidth‑density, multi‑terabit cable interconnects in data centers. Its Active RF Cable platform transmits data wirelessly using millimeter wave RF transmitter/receiver SoCs through a plastic dielectric waveguide. A purely electrical technology, the company says it can scale to 800G/1.6T/3.2T cable speeds and beyond by implementing modern modulation techniques for wireless transmission. Founded in 2016, it is based in San Jose, California, USA.</p>
<h2 id="Power-devices">Power devices</h2>
<p><strong>Reed Semiconductor</strong> raised <a href="https://www.reedsemi.com/reed-semiconductor-completes-100-million-funding-round/">$100.0M in funding</a>. Reed offers power semiconductors for a range of markets. Its products include multiphase controllers, smart power stages, power modules, intermediate bus converters, eFuses, power MUX, and point-of-load DC-DC controllers. Funds will be used to accelerate product development, broaden market reach, and enhance operational scale. Founded in 2019, it is based in Warwick, Rhode Island, USA.</p>
<p><strong>IVWorks</strong> raised <a href="https://www.ivwkr.com/mediacenter-post/ivworks-secures-4-5m-funding-to-expand-regan-technology-into-rf-and-ai-power-semiconductor-markets/?lang=en">$4.5M in pre-IPO funding</a>. IVWorks makes gallium nitride (GaN) epitaxial wafers for RF and power semiconductor applications. It offers a selective area regrowth capability for advanced GaN device integration to address contact resistance reduction in GaN devices. It also provides AlN HEMT on SiC, GaN HEMT on Si, and vertical GaN epiwafers. The company is expanding into E-band and W-band RF applications for satellite communications and wireless backhaul, as well as AI power delivery with PoL converters for HBM and GPU platforms. IVWorks is preparing to list on KOSDAQ. Founded in 2011, it is based in Daejeon, South Korea, and has raised $33M to date.</p>
<h2 id="Photonics">Photonics &amp; optics</h2>
<p><strong>HyperLight</strong> raised <a href="https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260618064171/en/HyperLight-Announces-%2480-Million-Series-C-to-Accelerate-TFLN-Deployment-for-AI-Infrastructure">$80.0M in Series C funding</a> led by <strong>MediaTek</strong>, with participation from <strong>UMC Capital</strong>, <strong>Jabil</strong>, <strong>Foxconn</strong>, <strong>SG Growth Capital</strong>, <strong>CDIB-TEN Capital</strong>, <strong>Qatar Investment Authority</strong>, and others. HyperLight provides <a href="https://hyperlightcorp.com">thin film lithium niobate</a> (TFLN) photonic ICs. Its TFLN chiplet platform is designed to unify the requirements of short-reach IMDD-based data center pluggables, longer-reach coherent datacom and telecom modules, and co-packaged optics within a single high-volume manufacturable architecture that combines ultra-high modulation bandwidth, CMOS-level drive voltage, and ultra-low optical loss. It currently supports 200G-per-lane operation and is sampling 400G-per-lane solutions. Funds will be used to expand manufacturing capacity, accelerate qualification, and scale the chiplet platform. Founded in 2018, it is based in Cambridge, Massachusetts, USA.</p>
<p><strong>nEye</strong> raised <a href="https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260414407496/en/nEye.ai-Secures-%2480-Million-Series-C-to-Scale-Optical-Circuit-Switching-for-AI-Infrastructure">$80.0M in Series C funding</a> led by <strong>Sutter Hill Ventures</strong> with participation from Alphabet&#8217;s <strong>CapitalG</strong>, Microsoft’s <strong>M12</strong>, <strong>Socratic Partners</strong>, and others. nEye develops <a href="https://www.neye.ai/">optical circuit switches</a> that integrate silicon photonics, MEMS, and CMOS in a single chip. Targeted at data centers and AI fabrics, the reconfigurable optical interconnect uses compact, fast-switching optical layers that allow for the flexible pooling of CPU, GPU, and memory resources. Funds will accelerate development and high-volume manufacturing. Founded in 2020, it is based in Santa Clara, California, USA, and has raised $152M to date.</p>
<p><strong>OpenLight</strong> raised <a href="https://openlightphotonics.com/newsroom/openlight-secures-50-million-in-series-a-1-funding-to-accelerate-global-deployment-of-next-generation-photonics">$50.0M in a Series A-1 round</a> led by <strong>Matter Venture Partners</strong>, with participation from <strong>Acclimate Ventures</strong>, <strong>Catapult Ventures</strong>, <strong>Xora Innovation</strong>, <strong>Capricorn Investment Group</strong>, <strong>Mayfield</strong>, and <strong>New Legacy Ventures</strong>. OpenLight enables custom photonic ASIC design and manufacturing through its process design kit (PDK). Based on the heterogeneous integration of indium phosphide and silicon photonics, the PDK provides access to a library of passive and active components covering integrated lasers, modulators, amplifiers, and detectors, including a 400Gb/s modulator and indium phosphide heterogeneously integrated on-chip laser technology. It also offers design services. Funds will be used to expand its PDK library and ramp up development of reference PICs at 1.6T and 3.2T. Formerly a Synopsys subsidiary, it became an independent company in 2025 and is based in Santa Barbara, California, USA.</p>
<h2 id="Sensors">Sensors</h2>
<p><strong>Eyeo</strong> raised <a href="https://eyeo-imaging.com/eyeo-raises-40-million-euros-series-a/">€40.0M (~$47.1M) in Series A funding</a> led by <strong>Innovation Industries</strong>, with participation from <strong>imec.xpand</strong>, <strong>Invest-NL</strong>, <strong>QBIC Fund</strong>, <strong>High-Tech Gründerfonds</strong>, and <strong>Brabant Development Agency</strong>. Eyeo offers a nanophotonic color-splitting technology for image sensors. Compatible with any CMOS sensor platform, the vertical waveguide-based technology splits light into component colors and guides photons to single pixels, which maximizes light sensitivity compared to traditional color filters, particularly in low-light environments. It also allows the sub-0.5-micron pixels to receive complete color data, increasing resolution. Funding will be used to accelerate sensor design capabilities, deepen OEM partner ecosystem, and drive commercial deployment. A spin-off from imec founded in 2024, it is based in Eindhoven, the Netherlands.</p>
<p><strong>Prophesee</strong> raised <a href="https://www.prophesee.ai/2026/06/15/prophesee-launches-mantara-event-based-drone-detection/">€20.0M (~$23.2M) in funding</a> led by <strong>Critical Path Ventures</strong>. Prophesee makes neuromorphic event-based image sensors. Inspired by the human visual system, each of the sensor&#8217;s pixels responds independently in microseconds when movement occurs, enabling it to capture fast, erratic targets in low light, backlit, and cluttered environments. The sensors are paired with a software platform with built-in AI processing. The company recently introduced an integrated drone detection and tracking system. It also targets industrial, consumer, and automotive applications. Founded in 2014, it is based in Paris, France.</p>

		<post-id xmlns="com-wordpress:feed-additions:1">24278774</post-id>	</item>
		<item>
		<title>Change Is Tough</title>
		<link>https://semiengineering.com/change-is-tough/</link>
					<comments>https://semiengineering.com/change-is-tough/#respond</comments>
		
		<dc:creator><![CDATA[Brian Bailey]]></dc:creator>
		<pubDate>Mon, 13 Jul 2026 07:05:06 +0000</pubDate>
				<category><![CDATA[Opinion]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[System-Level Design]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[What Were They Thinking]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[AI]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[business]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[EDA]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[ESL]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[IP]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[models]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[standards]]></category>
		<guid isPermaLink="false">https://semiengineering.com/?p=24278800</guid>

					<description><![CDATA[<p>There has never been a time when change has become something everyone expects. Now we are figuring out what it means to us individually.</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/change-is-tough/">Change Is Tough</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<p>When I was actively involved in the creation of standards for the EDA and semiconductor industry, it was often joked that the great thing about standards is that there are plenty to choose from. According to the Internet, this quote can either be attributed to Grace Murray Hopper (an incredible pioneer in the development of modern programming languages and a rear admiral in the Navy), or Andrew S. Tanenbaum (author of a foundational book on computers and operating systems), or several other people, although it can provide no evidence for any of these to be true. This is typical of many quotes — they just become convenient.</p>
<p>The reality is that it probably was first said by an unknown junior engineer when told they had to design something based on standards.</p>
<p><i>&#8220;One of the great things about adages, idioms, proverbs and other short witty statements is that there are so many of them you can get them to tell you anything you want.&#8221; — Brian Bailey (EDA developer and journalist, 2026)</i></p>
<p>Of course, that engineer would have picked the easiest standard to implement, would have finished in record time, and been quite proud of what he had accomplished.</p>
<p>The semiconductor industry has been notoriously conservative, and this is quite understandable. The cost of getting something wrong is huge. The industry has evolved by making the minimum changes along a single trajectory so that you get the advancements needed at the lowest cost, and more importantly, the lowest risk. It has worked incredibly well, as evidenced by how far technology has moved in the past 50 years.</p>
<p>We can find great quotes about this. Leo Tolstoy (Russian author) is often credited with the notion that meaningful progress often comes not from grand, complex reforms, but from small, straightforward adjustments that are easy to implement and sustain. It also could be a quote from Charles Lindblom (a social scientist from Yale).</p>
<p>That strategy works until you hit a brick wall. <i>&#8220;If you always do what you’ve always done, you’ll always get what you’ve always got.&#8221;</i> At times attributed to Henry Ford, it is now thought that this quote is false, and it may be attributable to Jessie Potter (educator and counselor, 1981). But it sounds so much better if associated with someone famous.</p>
<p>When faced with that brick wall, you either need to be able to smash through it or move around it. So far, smashing through it has always been the path of success. <i>&#8220;If you put enough money and effort into solving a small problem, you will get better results than putting the same amount of money and effort into solving a big problem.&#8221; — Brian Bailey.</i></p>
<p>I found that one out directly while attempting to develop the Electronic System Level (ESL) design flow. It was pushed aside with the development of reusable IP, which took a lot of time and effort to get established, but once it did, it required a bottom-up assembly methodology rather than a top-down flow.</p>
<p><i>&#8220;The world as we have created it is a process of our thinking. It cannot be changed without changing our thinking.&#8221;  — Albert Einstein.</i></p>
<p>The software industry has looked at risk differently. <i>&#8220;The biggest risk is not taking any risk. In a world that’s changing really quickly, the only strategy that is guaranteed to fail is not taking risks.&#8221; — Mark Zuckerberg.</i></p>
<p>This is often encapsulated by the notion of &#8216;run fast and fail,&#8217; which has existed at least since 1992 (Sim Sitkin of Duke University), and in the business world from at least 2001 (Carol Bartz). It really is the essence of every startup company that wants to quickly assess the viability of something before spending too much time and money on it. This is simple in the software world because it requires almost no capital, and time to product is fast, even if not efficient.</p>
<p>But the semiconductor industry is at a crossroads where the path forward is perhaps not so clear. AI is putting everything on the line. Can we expect product design cycles to be reduced? And why do we even want that? Am I expected to upgrade my phone every three months, or upgrade something because USB has become 5% faster? That would be a terrible direction for the industry to take. The amount of waste would be insufferable. <i>&#8220;A typical tech toy. High-end this year, everywhere next year, nowhere after that until the antiquarians revival.&#8221; — Lois McMaster Bujold (fiction writer).</i></p>
<p>Will AI give us better products? I would love to see products that consume 20% less power, for example, but who will pay for it? This may be true for battery-powered devices to some extent, but a device that needs charging every 4 days instead of every 3 days doesn&#8217;t seem to be that important. Would we do it for a cleaner, greener world? We might say we would, but how many actively make that decision today?  How many people do actually pay for renewable energy rather than dirty energy when given the choice? <i>&#8220;The stone age didn’t end because we ran out of stones. It ended because we had better ideas.&#8221; — attributed to Sheikh Zaki Yamani (Saudi oil minister), but highly disputed and may have been Don Huberts (Royal Dutch Shell).</i></p>
<p>What about devices that are of higher quality? Let&#8217;s face it, I wish I had never gotten rid of that 20-year-old refrigerator when I replaced it 10 years ago because it wasn&#8217;t pretty. Since then, three brand new fridges have failed on me. These are meant to be better products, but they are of throw-away quality, not just in their electronics, but in every aspect of them. When shown the option of something that may last longer, the price gap is huge. <i>&#8220;We live in a disposable, &#8216;cast-off and throw-away&#8217; society that has largely lost any real sense of permanence. Ours is a world of expiration dates, limited shelf life, and planned obsolescence. Nothing is absolute.&#8221; — Myles Munroe (religious author).</i></p>
<p>I will leave you with two quotes that perfectly sum up what is happening today. <i>&#8220;At least 40% of all businesses will die in the next 10 years… if they don’t figure out how to change their entire company to accommodate new technologies.&#8221; — John Chambers (CEO of Cisco, 2015).</i></p>
<p><i>&#8220;AI is not only for engineers. It brings changes in the dynamic of business, and we have to adapt or die.&#8221; — attributed to Satya Nadella (CEO of Microsoft, 2025) but may have been stylized for added punch.</i></p>
<p>I don’t expect anyone to remember my quotes, but who knows. What I can assure you is they are the only 100% correctly attributed quotes in this story. I am sure AI is listening and will record them, but in a few years, they too will become quotes attributed to more famous people, or changed to fit someone else&#8217;s narrative.</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/change-is-tough/">Change Is Tough</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></content:encoded>
					
					<wfw:commentRss>https://semiengineering.com/change-is-tough/feed/</wfw:commentRss>
			<slash:comments>0</slash:comments>
		
		
		<post-id xmlns="com-wordpress:feed-additions:1">24278800</post-id>	</item>
		<item>
		<title>Innovation First, AI Second: Lessons From SSN And The Future Of Test</title>
		<link>https://semiengineering.com/innovation-first-ai-second-lessons-from-ssn-and-the-future-of-test/</link>
					<comments>https://semiengineering.com/innovation-first-ai-second-lessons-from-ssn-and-the-future-of-test/#respond</comments>
		
		<dc:creator><![CDATA[Marc Hutner]]></dc:creator>
		<pubDate>Mon, 13 Jul 2026 07:02:37 +0000</pubDate>
				<category><![CDATA[Blogs - PTES]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Test For The Autonomous Age]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Test, Measurement and Analytics]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[agentic AI]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[AI]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[AI agents]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[design for test]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[DFT]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[in-system test]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[scan test]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Siemens EDA]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[SSN]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[streaming scan network]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[test]]></category>
		<guid isPermaLink="false">https://semiengineering.com/?p=24278795</guid>

					<description><![CDATA[<p>Human engineers created the breakthrough architecture; AI helps maximize its value.</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/innovation-first-ai-second-lessons-from-ssn-and-the-future-of-test/">Innovation First, AI Second: Lessons From SSN And The Future Of Test</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<p><strong>By Marc Hutner and Ron Press</strong></p>
<p>Artificial Intelligence is rapidly becoming pervasive in society and across semiconductor development processes. We have to be careful not to apply AI to solve optimizations and challenges of existing methods but pair with engineering innovation for evolutionary results. One example is the application of scan test data in multi-core designs. AI can optimize the device IO bandwidth allocation to each core based on the core pattern size. However, an engineering innovation of using a packetized bus for scan data delivery (SSN) resulted in a much more powerful solution. The SSN bus is unprecedented in its ability and flexibility to optimize packets in software for any changes in pattern size or combinations of cores to test in parallel. It also removed the dependency of core scan channel allocation and planning to available top-level IO. Consequently, SSN has quickly become the plan of record for SoC scan data delivery. Since its packetized high-speed bus provides so much flexibility, it has served as an infrastructure for several other new data delivery technologies.</p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" fetchpriority="high" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278796" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Siemens_Innovation-First-AI-Second-SSN-Test-fig1.jpg?resize=1071%2C714&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="1071" height="714" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Siemens_Innovation-First-AI-Second-SSN-Test-fig1.jpg?w=1071&amp;ssl=1 1071w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Siemens_Innovation-First-AI-Second-SSN-Test-fig1.jpg?resize=300%2C200&amp;ssl=1 300w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Siemens_Innovation-First-AI-Second-SSN-Test-fig1.jpg?resize=1024%2C683&amp;ssl=1 1024w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Siemens_Innovation-First-AI-Second-SSN-Test-fig1.jpg?resize=768%2C512&amp;ssl=1 768w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Siemens_Innovation-First-AI-Second-SSN-Test-fig1.jpg?resize=600%2C400&amp;ssl=1 600w" sizes="(max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /></p>
<p>We are providing Agentic AI capabilities that work with an engineer for DFT planning, optimizations, debug, and more later this year. For example, when a DFT tool encounters a design rule checking violation, the user often has to look up typical root causes and resolution and run experiments. An Agentic AI assistant can accelerate this process by:</p>
<ul>
<li>Identifying likely root causes from documentation and troubleshooting guides</li>
<li>Correlating violations with known design patterns</li>
<li>Executing diagnostic experiments automatically</li>
<li>Proposing potential fixes</li>
<li>Summarizing confidence levels and expected impact</li>
</ul>
<p>The engineer remains responsible for the final decision, but the AI dramatically reduces the time required to reach a solution. AI in combination with the packetized data delivery can provide very broad capabilities, bigger than DFT.</p>
<p>The next use case may be smarter ways to run tests and apply tests to improve operational awareness. A technology that was introduced a year ago was the In-System Test product. It provides the user with a way to run test content in-situ to the final application. In more basic terms, manufacturing tests run on the blade server and avoid doing an RMA. Imagine a data center with a thousand blade servers and a signal is detected by an agentic monitoring agent. This could be bus functional monitors, temperature sensors, voltage droop or some other kind of event. The agent can direct the scheduler to run a group of tests to determine how the hardware has changed. These tests are provided by the In-System Test hardware through an interface like PCIe. This may determine an action like a core needs to be taken out of service or that an entire blade server is no longer operating effectively.</p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278797" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Siemens_Innovation-First-AI-Second-SSN-Test-fig2.webp?resize=522%2C348&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="522" height="348" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Siemens_Innovation-First-AI-Second-SSN-Test-fig2.webp?w=522&amp;ssl=1 522w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Siemens_Innovation-First-AI-Second-SSN-Test-fig2.webp?resize=300%2C200&amp;ssl=1 300w" sizes="(max-width: 522px) 100vw, 522px" /></p>
<p>The future of DFT is not AI replacing engineers. Instead, it is AI amplifying engineering expertise. Human innovation will continue to create new test architectures, methodologies, and infrastructure, while AI accelerates optimization, analysis, and decision-making.</p>
<p>Together, they create a powerful partnership that extends beyond manufacturing test and into operational intelligence across the entire semiconductor lifecycle, from design and validation to deployment and field operation.</p>
<p>Some of the most impactful applications have yet to be imagined.</p>
<p><em>Ron Press is senior director of technology enablement at Siemens EDA.</em></p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/innovation-first-ai-second-lessons-from-ssn-and-the-future-of-test/">Innovation First, AI Second: Lessons From SSN And The Future Of Test</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></content:encoded>

		<pubDate>Mon, 13 Jul 2026 07:01:25 +0000</pubDate>
				<category><![CDATA[Blogs - LPHP]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[IP And LP In SoCs]]></category>

					<description><![CDATA[<p>Evolving Ethernet into a lossless, low-latency, deterministic transport built for the way AI accelerators actually talk to each other. </p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/rethinking-ethernet-for-the-ai-scale-up-era-inside-esun/">Rethinking Ethernet For The AI Scale-Up Era: Inside ESUN</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<p>Every generation of AI infrastructure has redefined what &#8220;the network&#8221; means. In today&#8217;s training clusters — scaling from hundreds to hundreds of thousands of accelerators — the interconnect is no longer a supporting actor. It has become a first-order determinant of system throughput, utilization, and cost per token. Accelerators inside these systems don&#8217;t just move data; they synchronize on it, step after step. When even a small fraction of that traffic is late, expensive compute sits idle waiting.</p>
<p>That reality is forcing a rethink of the fabric itself. The result is <a href="https://www.opencompute.org/wiki/Networking/ESUN">ESUN — Ethernet for Scale-Up Networking</a> — an open, <a href="https://www.opencompute.org/wiki/Networking/ESUN">OCP-defined</a> effort to evolve Ethernet into a lossless, low-latency, deterministic transport built for the way AI accelerators actually talk to each other.</p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278805" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Rethinking-Ethernet-AI-Scale-Up-ESUN-fig1.webp?resize=1200%2C675&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="1200" height="675" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Rethinking-Ethernet-AI-Scale-Up-ESUN-fig1.webp?w=1200&amp;ssl=1 1200w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Rethinking-Ethernet-AI-Scale-Up-ESUN-fig1.webp?resize=300%2C169&amp;ssl=1 300w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Rethinking-Ethernet-AI-Scale-Up-ESUN-fig1.webp?resize=1024%2C576&amp;ssl=1 1024w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Rethinking-Ethernet-AI-Scale-Up-ESUN-fig1.webp?resize=768%2C432&amp;ssl=1 768w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Rethinking-Ethernet-AI-Scale-Up-ESUN-fig1.webp?resize=600%2C338&amp;ssl=1 600w" sizes="(max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /></p>
<p><strong>Fig. 1: AI accelerator clusters need tightly orchestrated interconnects to scale efficiently.</strong></p>
<h2>The tail-latency problem: One slow packet stalls the cluster</h2>
<p>Classic Ethernet was engineered as a best-effort fabric. It tolerates occasional loss, retries, and jitter because most cloud workloads can absorb them. AI training cannot.</p>
<p>AI performance is gated by <strong>tail latency</strong> — the slowest packet in a collective operation. Even if 99% of traffic arrives on time, one delayed packet can stall an all-reduce and idle the entire cluster. Packet loss compounds the problem: retransmissions inject additional delay and variability that ripple across synchronized workloads.</p>
<p>At scale, these inefficiencies translate directly into wasted bandwidth, higher power draw, and rising infrastructure cost. When clusters grow to hundreds of thousands of accelerators, the math simply doesn&#8217;t hold.</p>
<p>From an architect&#8217;s point of view, the goal of a scale-up network is almost the opposite of what Ethernet was built for: the network should disappear. Accelerators should behave as if they&#8217;re inside a single, unified machine — communication that feels local rather than remote. That requires a fabric that is not only fast, but predictable, efficient, and tightly coupled to the compute domain.</p>
<h2>Where standard Ethernet falls short for scale-up</h2>
<p>Ethernet does include congestion tools — PFC and ECN — but these were designed for general-purpose data centers, not the tightly coupled, latency-sensitive traffic patterns of an AI pod. At scale-up distances and data rates, three limitations become structural:</p>
<ul>
<li><strong>Protocol overhead.</strong> Standard IP headers add 28–48 bytes per packet. In scale-up traffic, where messages are small and frequent, that overhead directly erodes usable throughput.</li>
<li><strong>No link-level error recovery.</strong> Beyond FEC, Ethernet leans on upper-layer transport to recover errors — an approach that costs orders of magnitude in latency.</li>
<li><strong>Coarse congestion control.</strong> PFC is a link-wide pause. It cannot distinguish between traffic classes or virtual channels, which leads to head-of-line blocking exactly when AI workloads can least afford it.</li>
</ul>
<p>These gaps have pushed some architectures toward proprietary interconnects. Those solve the immediate problem but bring their own baggage: ecosystem fragmentation, reduced flexibility, and long-term vendor risk.</p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278806" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Rethinking-Ethernet-AI-Scale-Up-ESUN-fig2.webp?resize=1200%2C675&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="1200" height="675" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Rethinking-Ethernet-AI-Scale-Up-ESUN-fig2.webp?w=1200&amp;ssl=1 1200w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Rethinking-Ethernet-AI-Scale-Up-ESUN-fig2.webp?resize=300%2C169&amp;ssl=1 300w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Rethinking-Ethernet-AI-Scale-Up-ESUN-fig2.webp?resize=1024%2C576&amp;ssl=1 1024w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Rethinking-Ethernet-AI-Scale-Up-ESUN-fig2.webp?resize=768%2C432&amp;ssl=1 768w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Rethinking-Ethernet-AI-Scale-Up-ESUN-fig2.webp?resize=600%2C338&amp;ssl=1 600w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /></p>
<p><strong>Fig. 2: ESUN vs standard Ethernet: Architectural enhancements.</strong></p>
<h2>ESUN: Evolving Ethernet, not replacing it</h2>
<p>Rather than starting over, the industry is adapting Ethernet where it needs to change and preserving what already works. That&#8217;s the core philosophy behind <a href="https://www.synopsys.com/designware-ip/interface-ip/ethernet/ethernet-scale-up-networking-esun.html">ESUN</a>.</p>
<p>What stays intact matters as much as what&#8217;s new:</p>
<ul>
<li><strong>Operational familiarity</strong> — network teams keep their existing Ethernet expertise, tooling, and workflows instead of learning a new fabric.</li>
<li><strong>Infrastructure reuse</strong> — scale-up and scale-out traffic share the same switching architecture and physical layer, keeping cost and complexity in check.</li>
<li><strong>One unified fabric</strong> — Ethernet becomes the common connective tissue across the AI data center, from inside the rack to across the campus.</li>
</ul>
<p>The enhancements themselves are surgical:</p>
<ul>
<li>The physical layer remains standard Ethernet.</li>
<li>The link layer adds lossless reliability and fine-grained congestion control.</li>
<li>The network layer is streamlined to strip out overhead the scale-up domain doesn&#8217;t need.</li>
</ul>
<p>The net effect: AI systems get Ethernet&#8217;s scale, interoperability, and ecosystem economics without inheriting assumptions written for a different era.</p>
<h2>A smaller header with outsized impact</h2>
<p>The most visible change is at the packet itself. Where standard Ethernet carries 28–48 bytes of IP header overhead, ESUN replaces it with a compact 4-byte header. In a workload dominated by small, frequent messages, that difference translates directly into higher effective bandwidth and lower per-packet latency.</p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278807" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Rethinking-Ethernet-AI-Scale-Up-ESUN-fig3.webp?resize=1200%2C675&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="1200" height="675" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Rethinking-Ethernet-AI-Scale-Up-ESUN-fig3.webp?w=1200&amp;ssl=1 1200w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Rethinking-Ethernet-AI-Scale-Up-ESUN-fig3.webp?resize=300%2C169&amp;ssl=1 300w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Rethinking-Ethernet-AI-Scale-Up-ESUN-fig3.webp?resize=1024%2C576&amp;ssl=1 1024w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Rethinking-Ethernet-AI-Scale-Up-ESUN-fig3.webp?resize=768%2C432&amp;ssl=1 768w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Rethinking-Ethernet-AI-Scale-Up-ESUN-fig3.webp?resize=600%2C338&amp;ssl=1 600w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /></p>
<p><strong>Fig. 3: IP header vs. ESUN header.</strong></p>
<p>Nothing critical is lost in the trim. Traffic prioritization, congestion signaling, and load balancing are all still there — just implemented more efficiently for the scale-up domain.</p>
<h2>Engineering for predictable, lossless communication</h2>
<p>ESUN introduces two mechanisms that push determinism down into the link layer, where it belongs for scale-up:</p>
<ul>
<li><strong>Link-Level Retry (LLR)</strong> detects and recovers from link errors locally at the data link layer, rather than punting recovery up the stack. That drops tail latency dramatically and avoids costly end-to-end retransmission.</li>
<li><strong>Credit-Based Flow Control (CBFC)</strong> replaces PFC&#8217;s blunt link-wide pause with per-virtual-channel flow control. Senders only transmit when the receiver has confirmed buffer capacity, delivering lossless behavior without head-of-line blocking.</li>
</ul>
<p>Together, LLR and CBFC give ESUN its signature profile: lossless, low-latency, bandwidth-efficient — with Ethernet&#8217;s openness and interoperability intact.</p>
<h2>A complete ESUN IP solution</h2>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278808" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Rethinking-Ethernet-AI-Scale-Up-ESUN-fig4.webp?resize=1200%2C675&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="1200" height="675" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Rethinking-Ethernet-AI-Scale-Up-ESUN-fig4.webp?w=1200&amp;ssl=1 1200w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Rethinking-Ethernet-AI-Scale-Up-ESUN-fig4.webp?resize=300%2C169&amp;ssl=1 300w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Rethinking-Ethernet-AI-Scale-Up-ESUN-fig4.webp?resize=1024%2C576&amp;ssl=1 1024w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Rethinking-Ethernet-AI-Scale-Up-ESUN-fig4.webp?resize=768%2C432&amp;ssl=1 768w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Rethinking-Ethernet-AI-Scale-Up-ESUN-fig4.webp?resize=600%2C338&amp;ssl=1 600w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /></p>
<p><strong>Fig. 4: ESUN solution.</strong></p>
<p>To help SoC teams move from spec to silicon quickly, Synopsys has introduced the <a href="https://www.synopsys.com/designware-ip/interface-ip/ethernet/ethernet-scale-up-networking-esun.html">industry&#8217;s first complete ESUN IP solution</a> spanning both Layer 1 and Layer 2 — a pre-verified stack designed to work together out of the box.</p>
<p>The solution includes:</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="https://www.synopsys.com/designware-ip/interface-ip/ethernet/1-6t-ethernet-mac-ip.html"><strong>1.6T multi-rate Ethernet MAC</strong></a> — supporting up to four independent 400G channels, configurable for 1×1.6T, 2×800G, 4×400G, and 4×200G modes over 224G SerDes</li>
<li><a href="https://www.synopsys.com/designware-ip/interface-ip/ethernet/1-6t-ethernet-pcs-ip.html"><strong>1.6T Ethernet PCS with RS-FEC</strong></a> — RS544 for robust error correction and RS272 for low-latency operation</li>
<li><strong>UE/ESUN Link Layer Controller</strong> — implementing LLR and CBFC for lossless reliability and fine-grained congestion management</li>
<li><a href="https://www.synopsys.com/designware-ip/interface-ip/ethernet/224g-ethernet-phy.html"><strong>Silicon-proven 224G Ethernet PHY</strong></a> — optimized for low power, latency, and signal integrity</li>
<li><strong>Comprehensive Verification IP</strong> and system-level validation support, including an ESUN-ready SoC Verification Kit (SVK)</li>
</ul>
<p>By delivering the full stack, Synopsys collapses much of the integration burden — and integration risk — that typically comes with assembling high-speed networking IP from multiple sources.</p>
<h3>Built for integration, performance, and scale</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>End-to-end integration.</strong> MAC, PCS, PHY, and the UE/ESUN Link Layer Controller are co-designed and validated as a single system, eliminating the multi-vendor stitching that so often derails schedules late in a program.</li>
<li><strong>One architecture for scale-up and scale-out.</strong> The same IP addresses both <a href="https://www.synopsys.com/designware-ip/interface-ip/ethernet/ethernet-scale-up-networking-esun.html">ESUN (scale-up)</a> and <a href="https://www.synopsys.com/designware-ip/interface-ip/ethernet/ultra-ethernet.html">Ultra Ethernet (scale-out)</a>, letting teams reuse a common architecture across different parts of the data center.</li>
<li><strong>Optimized for real-world constraints.</strong> The solution targets low power, high performance, and area efficiency, with multi-rate configurations from 100G to 1.6T and flexible FEC modes for varied system requirements.</li>
<li><strong>A proven PHY foundation.</strong> At the core is the <a href="https://www.synopsys.com/designware-ip/interface-ip/ethernet/224g-ethernet-phy.html">Synopsys 224G PHY</a>, in production across multiple advanced process nodes. Since October 2022, Synopsys has publicly demonstrated 224G silicon interoperability in more than 30 multi-vendor showings at ECOC and OFC, delivering zero post-FEC errors over channels with up to 45 dB of loss — evidence that what works on paper holds up in silicon.</li>
</ul>
<h2>Looking ahead: A network built for AI</h2>
<p>As AI models continue to scale, the demands on the fabric will only intensify. Networks will need to be fast — but also predictable, efficient, and scalable enough to keep hundreds of thousands of accelerators productively busy. Backed by more than 175 companies, including the leading hyperscalers and silicon vendors, ESUN represents the point at which Ethernet converges on that reality.</p>
<p>Synopsys&#8217; ESUN IP solution is one piece of a broader HPC IP portfolio built to support the full AI system stack:</p>
<ul>
<li>Scale-up connectivity with <a href="https://www.synopsys.com/designware-ip/interface-ip/ethernet/ethernet-scale-up-networking-esun.html">ESUN</a> and <a href="https://www.synopsys.com/designware-ip/interface-ip/ualink.html">UALink</a></li>
<li>Chip-to-chip connectivity with <a href="https://www.synopsys.com/designware-ip/interface-ip/pci-express.html">PCIe</a> and <a href="https://www.synopsys.com/designware-ip/interface-ip/cxl.html">CXL</a></li>
<li>Scale-out networking with <a href="https://www.synopsys.com/designware-ip/interface-ip/ethernet.html">Ethernet</a> and <a href="https://www.synopsys.com/designware-ip/interface-ip/ethernet/ultra-ethernet.html">Ultra Ethernet</a></li>
<li>High-bandwidth memory interfaces</li>
<li>Multi-die and chiplet interconnects</li>
<li>Security and Foundation IP</li>
</ul>
<p>Together, these building blocks give architects a foundation where compute, memory, and connectivity scale in lockstep — which is exactly what the next generation of AI infrastructure will demand.</p>
<h3>Learn more</h3>
<ul>
<li><a href="https://www.synopsys.com/designware-ip/interface-ip/ethernet/ethernet-scale-up-networking-esun.html">Visit the Synopsys ESUN IP Solution page</a> — full overview of the industry&#8217;s first complete L1+L2 ESUN IP.</li>
<li><a href="https://www.synopsys.com/resources/esun-ip-solution-datasheet.html">Download the ESUN IP Solution datasheet</a> — full architecture, features, and supported configurations.</li>
</ul>

<p>“In this work, we present an “Open DRAM Model” that enables comprehensive circuit-level analysis of DRAM operations across multiple architectures, including conventional 6F2 BCAT, scaled 4F2 VCT, and monolithically stacked 3-D DRAM. ”</p>

		<title>Hardware Abstraction Layer Study Targets Software-Defined Vehicles (U. of Stuttgart)</title>
		<link>https://semiengineering.com/hardware-abstraction-layer-study-targets-software-defined-vehicles-u-of-stuttgart/</link>
					<comments>https://semiengineering.com/hardware-abstraction-layer-study-targets-software-defined-vehicles-u-of-stuttgart/#respond</comments>
		
		<dc:creator><![CDATA[Technical Paper Link]]></dc:creator>
		<pubDate>Sun, 12 Jul 2026 17:41:56 +0000</pubDate>
				<category><![CDATA[Architectures]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Automotive/ Aerospace]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Design & Verification]]></category>

		<category><![CDATA[automotive software architecture]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[automotive validation]]></category>

					<description><![CDATA[<p>Researchers from University of Stuttgart published a technical paper titled “Evaluating Hardware Abstraction Layer Concepts for Software Defined Vehicles: Insights into Applicability and Effectiveness.” Abstract Excerpt: “The emergence of Software-Defined Vehicles represents a fundamental shift in automotive design, prioritizing software-centric architectures over traditional hardware-driven models. SDVs require modularity, interoperability, real-time processing, and over-the-air update capabilities... <a class="read_more" href="https://semiengineering.com/hardware-abstraction-layer-study-targets-software-defined-vehicles-u-of-stuttgart/">&#187; read more</a></p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/hardware-abstraction-layer-study-targets-software-defined-vehicles-u-of-stuttgart/">Hardware Abstraction Layer Study Targets Software-Defined Vehicles (U. of Stuttgart)</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<p>Researchers from University of Stuttgart published a technical paper titled “Evaluating Hardware Abstraction Layer Concepts for Software Defined Vehicles: Insights into Applicability and Effectiveness.”</p>
<p><strong>Abstract Excerpt:</strong></p>
<p>“The emergence of Software-Defined Vehicles represents a fundamental shift in automotive design, prioritizing software-centric architectures over traditional hardware-driven models. SDVs require modularity, interoperability, real-time processing, and over-the-air update capabilities throughout the vehicle lifecycle. However, current vehicle systems, characterized by tightly coupled software and hardware, struggle to meet these demands due to their complexity and heterogeneity. A critical first step toward enabling SDVs is the decoupling of software from hardware, which can be facilitated through a robust Hardware Abstraction Layer. While existing HALs offer hardware independence and standardized interfaces, their applicability and effectiveness in SDV contexts remain uncertain. This paper systematically evaluates current automotive HALs and explores HAL mechanisms from non-automotive domains, including smartphones, networking, and industrial automation, to extract cross-domain insights relevant to SDV development. &#8220;</p>
<p>Find the technical paper <a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00039">here</a>. June 2026.</p>
<p>Narla, Akshay, Johannes Stümpfle, Souvik Saha, Nasser Jazdi, and Michael Weyrich. &#8220;Evaluating Hardware Abstraction Layer Concepts for Software Defined Vehicles: Insights into Applicability and Effectiveness.&#8221; In 2025 IEEE International Automated Vehicle Validation Conference (IAVVC), pp. 1-6. IEEE, 2025.</p>
<p>&nbsp;</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/hardware-abstraction-layer-study-targets-software-defined-vehicles-u-of-stuttgart/">Hardware Abstraction Layer Study Targets Software-Defined Vehicles (U. of Stuttgart)</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></content:encoded>

		<title>3nm GAA-FET SRAM Review Evaluates Self-Heating And Radiation Hardness (SJSU, Sandia)</title>
		<link>https://semiengineering.com/3nm-gaa-fet-sram-review-evaluates-self-heating-and-radiation-hardness-sjsu-sandia/</link>
					<comments>https://semiengineering.com/3nm-gaa-fet-sram-review-evaluates-self-heating-and-radiation-hardness-sjsu-sandia/#respond</comments>
		
		<dc:creator><![CDATA[Technical Paper Link]]></dc:creator>
		<pubDate>Sun, 12 Jul 2026 17:35:02 +0000</pubDate>
				<category><![CDATA[Design & Verification]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Manufacturing]]></category>

		<guid isPermaLink="false">https://semiengineering.com/?p=24278825</guid>

					<description><![CDATA[<p>Researchers from San Jose State University and Sandia National Laboratories published a technical paper titled “Self-Heating and Radiation Hardness Studies of 3nm GAA-FET-Based SRAM with Different Substrate Isolation Techniques.” Abstract Excerpt: “In addition to the traditional bottom dielectric isolation (BDI), which isolates the source/drain (S/D) from the substrate (dubbed SD-BDI), and the punch-through stopper (PTS),... <a class="read_more" href="https://semiengineering.com/3nm-gaa-fet-sram-review-evaluates-self-heating-and-radiation-hardness-sjsu-sandia/">&#187; read more</a></p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/3nm-gaa-fet-sram-review-evaluates-self-heating-and-radiation-hardness-sjsu-sandia/">3nm GAA-FET SRAM Review Evaluates Self-Heating And Radiation Hardness (SJSU, Sandia)</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<p>Researchers from San Jose State University and Sandia National Laboratories published a technical paper titled “Self-Heating and Radiation Hardness Studies of 3nm GAA-FET-Based SRAM with Different Substrate Isolation Techniques.”</p>
<p><strong>Abstract Excerpt:</strong> “In addition to the traditional bottom dielectric isolation (BDI), which isolates the source/drain (S/D) from the substrate (dubbed SD-BDI), and the punch-through stopper (PTS), a novel channel-BDI (C-BDI) is proposed, allowing S/D-to-substrate connection.” The paper also reports that “all structures are immune to the alpha-particle SEU, and BDI enhances the radiation hardness substantially.”</p>
<p>Find the technical paper <a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.05789">here</a>. July 2026.</p>
<p>Lu, Albert, Junipero Verbeke, Phil Oldiges, Reza Arghavani, and Hiu Yung Wong. “Self-Heating and Radiation Hardness Studies of 3nm GAA-FET-Based SRAM with Different Substrate Isolation Techniques.” arXiv, July 2026. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2607.05789.</p>
<p>&nbsp;</p>
<p>&nbsp;</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/3nm-gaa-fet-sram-review-evaluates-self-heating-and-radiation-hardness-sjsu-sandia/">3nm GAA-FET SRAM Review Evaluates Self-Heating And Radiation Hardness (SJSU, Sandia)</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>

										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<p>Researchers from MITRE, University of Colorado Boulder, Sandia National Laboratories, University of Arizona, and MIT published a technical paper titled “Monolithic Integration of Piezo-Optomechanical Photonics and CMOS Electronics.”</p>
<p><strong>Abstract Excerpt:</strong> The paper demonstrates a “fully monolithic, all-CMOS fabricated platform” for piezo-optomechanical photonic integrated circuits and shows “wafer-scale integration of POMPICs on an electronic backplane.”</p>
<p>Find the technical paper <a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01514">here</a>. July 2026.</p>
<p>Zimmermann, Matthew, Aileen Zhai, Andrew J. Leenheer, Julia Boyle, Mayank Mishra, Daniel Dominguez, Matthew Koppa, Wolf Jehle, Christopher Panuski, Mark Dong, Gerald Gilbert, Dirk Englund, and Matt Eichenfield. “Monolithic Integration of Piezo-Optomechanical Photonics and CMOS Electronics.” arXiv, July 2026. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2607.01514.</p>

					<description><![CDATA[<p>Researchers from Rotonium, Centre for Quantum Technologies at National University of Singapore, Inveriant, Politecnico di Milano, and CNIT published a technical paper titled “Design and Benchmarking of a Quantum Photonic Chip.” Abstract Excerpt: The paper presents a quantum photonic processor based on “standard CMOS-compatible manufacturing processes” and operating with single photons at room temperature. The... <a class="read_more" href="https://semiengineering.com/room-temperature-cmos-compatible-photonic-quantum-processor-nus-et-al/">&#187; read more</a></p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/room-temperature-cmos-compatible-photonic-quantum-processor-nus-et-al/">Room-temperature, CMOS-compatible Photonic Quantum Processor (NUS et al.)</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<p>Researchers from Rotonium, Centre for Quantum Technologies at National University of Singapore, Inveriant, Politecnico di Milano, and CNIT published a technical paper titled “Design and Benchmarking of a Quantum Photonic Chip.”</p>
<p><strong>Abstract Excerpt:</strong> The paper presents a quantum photonic processor based on “standard CMOS-compatible manufacturing processes” and operating with single photons at room temperature. The authors also report “superior noise tolerance” compared with a superconducting quantum processor.</p>
<p>Find the technical paper <a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.06488">here</a>. July 2026.</p>
<p>De Angelis, Gabriele, Nicolò Leone, Alessandro Luongo, Alberto Montanaro, Matteo Sanna, Roberto Siagri, Vito Sorianello, Luigi Tallone, Fabrizio Tamburini, and Marco Venere. &#8220;Design and Benchmarking of a Quantum Photonic Chip.&#8221; arXiv preprint arXiv:2607.06488 (2026).</p>
